mnemonics.title: Subspace Test - ZAC
mnemonics.content:
Z - Zero vector must be present
A - Additive closure: u+v ∈ W
C - Closure under scalar multiplication: c·v ∈ W
Remember: ZAC = Zero Addition Closure

mnemonics.title: Trivial Always Subspace
mnemonics.content:
T - Two trivial subspaces always exist: {0} and V itself
A - Any line through origin is a subspace
S - Subspaces are closed under intersection
Remember: TAS = Trivial Always Subspace

acronyms.expansion: Zero vector, Additive closure, Closure under scalar multiplication
acronyms.usage: The three conditions to verify a subset is a subspace
A set that is both linearly independent and spans a subspace is a basis
U∩W = common elements; U+W = all sums; U⊕W = unique sums when U∩W = {0}

visualTips.concept: Subspace as a plane/line through origin
visualTips.visualization: Imagine R³ as a 3D space. Subspaces appear as: point at origin (0D), line through origin (1D), plane through origin (2D), or entire space (3D). Visualize them all passing through origin like spokes and sheets through center.

Think of span as taking a vector and 'stretching' it in all directions by scalar multiplication. With multiple vectors, imagine all possible combinations of stretching and adding them together - fills up a space like combining building blocks.

Picture two subspaces U and W intersecting only at origin like perpendicular axes in a plane. Their direct sum fills the entire space without gaps or overlaps - like partitioning a floor into non-overlapping tiles.

analogies.analogy: A subspace is like a club within a larger club. It has its own members (vectors) and follows the same rules (addition, scalar multiplication), but only includes a specific group - like how the 'secretaries' form a subset of all employees but still do the same work.

Span is like a recipe. Given ingredients (vectors), the span is all possible dishes (linear combinations) you can make using any amounts. One ingredient gives you variations of one dish; multiple ingredients give infinite recipes.

Direct sum is like having two non-overlapping teams. Team U has its own members, Team W has its own, and together they cover all projects uniquely. No one does the same work twice because they have no members in common except the supervisor (zero vector).
